title: The Rise of Cycling Cities
summary: A narrator examines how several cities around the world have transformed their streets to prioritise cycling, exploring the environmental, health, and social benefits of building cycling infrastructure.
body: Over the past two decades, a quiet revolution has been taking place on the streets of many of the world's major cities. Governments and urban planners have begun to rethink how people move through their cities, and cycling has emerged as one of the most promising solutions to a range of modern challenges. Amsterdam and Copenhagen have long been celebrated as models for cycling culture. In these cities, more than half of all daily trips are made by bicycle. Dedicated cycle lanes, traffic signals designed for cyclists, and secure parking facilities have made cycling not only convenient but genuinely enjoyable. Children cycle to school, office workers cycle to meetings, and elderly residents cycle to the market. Cycling is not a special activity for enthusiasts — it is simply how people get around. The success of these northern European cities has inspired many others to follow suit. In recent years, cities from Bogotá to Seville and from London to Taipei have invested heavily in cycling infrastructure. Some have built protected cycle paths separated from motor traffic by physical barriers. Others have introduced bike-sharing schemes, allowing residents and visitors to pick up a bicycle at one station and leave it at another. The results have been striking. In Seville, the introduction of a cycling network led to a tenfold increase in cycling trips within just a few years. In cities where cycling has been actively promoted, levels of air pollution have fallen, road congestion has decreased, and public health has improved. Regular cycling reduces the risk of heart disease, diabetes, and depression. Perhaps most surprisingly, studies have shown that towns and cities with high cycling rates tend to have stronger local economies. Cyclists stop more frequently at local shops than drivers do, and the money saved on car ownership is often spent locally. There are, of course, challenges. Not every city has the flat terrain of Amsterdam or the mild climate of Seville. Hills, rain, and extreme heat can all discourage cycling. Safety remains a concern in cities where cars and bicycles still share roads without adequate protection. Yet the evidence increasingly suggests that if cities invest in the right infrastructure, people will cycle. The shift may not happen overnight, but it has already begun in dozens of cities across the globe.

annotations.exampleEn: A quiet revolution in education has changed how children learn.

annotations.meaningVi: các nhà quy hoạch đô thị
annotations.exampleEn: Urban planners are designing greener cities for the future.

annotations.meaningVi: làn đường dành riêng cho xe đạp
annotations.exampleEn: The city has built dedicated cycle lanes along all major roads.
When one country banned plastic bags, others followed suit.
The government has invested heavily in renewable energy.

annotations.meaningVi: đường dành cho xe đạp được bảo vệ
annotations.exampleEn: Protected cycle paths make cycling safer for all ages.

annotations.meaningVi: chương trình chia sẻ xe đạp
annotations.exampleEn: Bike-sharing schemes are now common in most major cities.
There has been a tenfold increase in electric vehicle sales.
Road congestion is one of the biggest problems in modern cities.
Regular exercise can reduce the risk of heart disease.
Supporting local businesses helps strengthen local economies.
The flat terrain makes the region ideal for cycling.

annotations.meaningVi: nản lòng người đi xe đạp, khiến người ta không muốn đi xe đạp
annotations.exampleEn: Steep hills can discourage cycling in mountainous cities.
All cyclists should wear helmets for adequate protection.

annotations.meaningVi: sự thay đổi không thể xảy ra ngay lập tức
annotations.exampleEn: Cultural change may not happen overnight, but progress is visible.
Rising fuel costs have made car ownership more expensive.

grammarNotes.title: Present Perfect — Describing ongoing change and recent developments
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài dùng thì hiện tại hoàn thành để nói về những thay đổi đã bắt đầu trong quá khứ và vẫn còn liên quan đến hiện tại. Ví dụ: 'a quiet revolution has been taking place', 'cycling has emerged', 'cities have invested heavily'. Cấu trúc: have/has + past participle. Khác với quá khứ đơn (đã xong), thì này nhấn mạnh tính liên tục và kết quả hiện tại.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The present perfect is used to describe changes that began in the past and are still relevant now. Examples: 'a quiet revolution has been taking place', 'cycling has emerged', 'cities have invested heavily'. Structure: have/has + past participle. Unlike the simple past (which describes finished events), this tense emphasises ongoing relevance.

grammarNotes.title: Conditional Type 1 — Real possibilities in the future
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu điều kiện loại 1 được dùng để diễn đạt kết quả có thể xảy ra trong thực tế. Ví dụ: 'if cities invest in the right infrastructure, people will cycle'. Cấu trúc: If + present simple, will + bare infinitive. Loại điều kiện này cho thấy người nói tin rằng kết quả có thể thực sự xảy ra nếu điều kiện được đáp ứng.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The first conditional describes realistic possibilities. Example: 'if cities invest in the right infrastructure, people will cycle'. Structure: If + present simple, will + bare infinitive. This shows the speaker believes the outcome is genuinely possible if the condition is met.

translationVi:
Trong hai thập kỷ qua, một cuộc cách mạng lặng lẽ đã diễn ra trên các con phố của nhiều thành phố lớn trên thế giới. Các chính phủ và nhà quy hoạch đô thị đã bắt đầu suy nghĩ lại về cách người dân di chuyển trong thành phố, và đi xe đạp đã nổi lên như một trong những giải pháp hứa hẹn nhất cho nhiều thách thức hiện đại.

Amsterdam và Copenhagen từ lâu đã được ca ngợi là những mô hình cho văn hóa đi xe đạp. Ở những thành phố này, hơn một nửa số chuyến đi hàng ngày được thực hiện bằng xe đạp. Làn đường dành riêng cho xe đạp, đèn giao thông thiết kế cho người đi xe đạp, và bãi đỗ xe an toàn đã khiến việc đi xe đạp không chỉ tiện lợi mà còn thực sự thú vị. Trẻ em đạp xe đến trường, nhân viên văn phòng đạp xe đến các cuộc họp, và cư dân lớn tuổi đạp xe ra chợ. Đi xe đạp không phải là hoạt động đặc biệt cho những người đam mê — đó đơn giản là cách mọi người di chuyển.

Thành công của những thành phố Bắc Âu này đã truyền cảm hứng cho nhiều nơi khác noi theo. Trong những năm gần đây, các thành phố từ Bogotá đến Seville và từ London đến Taipei đã đầu tư mạnh vào cơ sở hạ tầng xe đạp. Một số đã xây dựng làn đường xe đạp được bảo vệ tách biệt với giao thông xe cơ giới bằng các rào chắn vật lý. Một số khác đã triển khai các chương trình chia sẻ xe đạp, cho phép cư dân và khách du lịch lấy xe đạp tại một trạm và trả ở trạm khác.

Kết quả rất ấn tượng. Ở Seville, việc triển khai mạng lưới xe đạp đã dẫn đến việc số chuyến đi bằng xe đạp tăng lên gấp mười lần chỉ trong vài năm. Ở những thành phố mà xe đạp được tích cực quảng bá, mức độ ô nhiễm không khí đã giảm, tắc nghẽn giao thông giảm bớt, và sức khỏe cộng đồng được cải thiện. Đi xe đạp thường xuyên giảm nguy cơ mắc bệnh tim, tiểu đường và trầm cảm. Có lẽ điều đáng ngạc nhiên nhất, các nghiên cứu đã chỉ ra rằng các thị trấn và thành phố có tỷ lệ đi xe đạp cao có xu hướng có nền kinh tế địa phương mạnh hơn. Người đi xe đạp ghé vào các cửa hàng địa phương thường xuyên hơn người lái xe, và số tiền tiết kiệm được từ việc không sở hữu xe hơi thường được chi tiêu tại địa phương.

Tất nhiên, cũng có những thách thức. Không phải mọi thành phố đều có địa hình bằng phẳng như Amsterdam hay khí hậu ôn hòa như Seville. Đồi núi, mưa và nắng nóng cực đoan đều có thể làm nản lòng người đi xe đạp. An toàn vẫn là mối lo ngại ở những thành phố mà xe hơi và xe đạp vẫn còn đi chung đường mà không có sự bảo vệ đầy đủ. Tuy nhiên, bằng chứng ngày càng cho thấy nếu các thành phố đầu tư vào cơ sở hạ tầng phù hợp, người dân sẽ đi xe đạp. Sự thay đổi có thể không xảy ra ngay lập tức, nhưng nó đã bắt đầu ở hàng chục thành phố trên toàn cầu.

questions.prompt: Which cities are described as long-established models for cycling culture?
questions.explanation: The passage says 'Amsterdam and Copenhagen have long been celebrated as models for cycling culture.'
questions.hint: The passage mentions these two cities first as examples.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what proportion of daily trips in Amsterdam and Copenhagen are made by bicycle?
questions.explanation: The passage states that 'more than half of all daily trips are made by bicycle' in these cities.
questions.hint: Think about the fraction mentioned in the passage.

questions.prompt: What are bike-sharing schemes described as allowing people to do?
options: Rent bicycles for a fixed monthly fee
Pick up a bicycle at one station and leave it at another
Buy second-hand bicycles at low cost
questions.explanation: The passage says bike-sharing schemes allow people to 'pick up a bicycle at one station and leave it at another.'
questions.hint: Think about the flexibility that makes bike-sharing different from simply renting.

questions.prompt: By how much did cycling trips increase in Seville after the cycling network was introduced?
questions.explanation: The passage says 'the introduction of a cycling network led to a tenfold increase in cycling trips within just a few years.'

questions.prompt: Which health conditions does the passage say regular cycling reduces the risk of?
options: Back pain, arthritis, and heart disease
Cancer, high blood pressure, and anxiety
questions.explanation: The passage lists 'heart disease, diabetes, and depression' as conditions that regular cycling helps prevent.
questions.hint: The passage gives three specific conditions.

questions.prompt: How do cyclists benefit local economies compared to drivers, according to the passage?
options: Cyclists stop more frequently at local shops
Cyclists earn higher incomes on average
questions.explanation: The passage says 'cyclists stop more frequently at local shops than drivers do.'
questions.hint: Think about the difference in shopping behaviour between cyclists and drivers.

questions.prompt: What physical challenges can discourage cycling in some cities?
options: Lack of public interest and political will
questions.explanation: The passage mentions 'hills, rain, and extreme heat' as environmental factors that can put people off cycling.
questions.hint: The passage mentions three natural/weather challenges.

questions.prompt: What does the passage suggest is the key factor needed for cycling to increase in any city?
options: A flat landscape and pleasant weather
Investment in the right cycling infrastructure
Strong government laws against car use
questions.explanation: The passage concludes: 'if cities invest in the right infrastructure, people will cycle.'
questions.hint: Look at the conditional sentence near the end of the passage.

questions.prompt: What does the narrator mean when they describe a 'quiet revolution' taking place in cities?
options: A revolution that is happening secretly
A gradual but significant change in how cities function
questions.explanation: 'Quiet revolution' is a metaphor meaning a major change that is happening gradually and without dramatic events.
questions.hint: The word 'quiet' here suggests the change is gradual rather than loud or sudden.

questions.prompt: Which of the following cities is NOT mentioned in the passage as having invested in cycling?
questions.explanation: The passage mentions Bogotá, Seville, London, and Taipei, but not Tokyo.
questions.hint: Check the list of cities mentioned in the third paragraph carefully.

questions.prompt: What does the passage say about the money people save by not owning a car?
options: It is usually saved in a bank
It is spent on public transport instead
It is used to buy better bicycles
questions.explanation: The passage says 'the money saved on car ownership is often spent locally.'
questions.hint: The passage connects car-free living with local economic activity.

cloze.template: Over the past two decades, a quiet revolution has been taking place on the streets of many of the world's major cities. Governments and urban planners have begun to {{b1}} how people move through their cities, and cycling has emerged as one of the most promising solutions to a range of modern challenges. Amsterdam and Copenhagen have long been celebrated as {{b2}} for cycling culture. In these cities, more than half of all daily trips are made by bicycle. Dedicated cycle lanes, traffic signals designed for cyclists, and secure parking {{b3}} have made cycling not only convenient but genuinely enjoyable. The success of these northern European cities has inspired many others to follow {{b4}}. In recent years, cities from Bogotá to Seville and from London to Taipei have invested heavily in cycling {{b5}}. Some have built protected cycle paths separated from motor traffic by physical barriers. Others have introduced bike-sharing schemes, allowing residents and visitors to pick up a bicycle at one station and leave it at another. The results have been {{b6}}. In Seville, the introduction of a cycling network led to a tenfold {{b7}} in cycling trips within just a few years. Regular cycling reduces the risk of heart disease, diabetes, and depression. Studies have shown that towns and cities with high cycling rates tend to have stronger local {{b8}}. Cyclists stop more frequently at local shops than drivers do, and the money saved on car {{b9}} is often spent locally. There are, of course, challenges. Not every city has the flat terrain of Amsterdam or the mild climate of Seville. Hills, rain, and extreme heat can all {{b10}} cycling. Yet the evidence increasingly suggests that if cities invest in the right infrastructure, people will cycle.

blanks.explanation: 'Rethink' means to think about something again in a new way — the exact word used in the passage.
'Celebrated as models' — 'models' here means examples that others should copy or be inspired by.
'Parking facilities' is the collocation used — 'facilities' refers to the equipment and infrastructure provided for a purpose.
'Follow suit' is an idiom meaning to do the same thing as someone else, especially after seeing their success.
'Cycling infrastructure' refers to the physical structures and systems built to support cycling.
'Striking' means noticeably impressive or remarkable — a stronger descriptor than simply 'positive'.
'Tenfold increase' — 'increase' is the noun used with 'tenfold' to describe a multiplication by ten.
'Local economies' refers to the financial activity in a specific area — the passage links cycling to economic strength.
'Car ownership' refers to the ongoing costs and responsibilities of owning a car, not just buying one.
'Discourage cycling' means to make people less likely to cycle — a weaker effect than 'prevent'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The passage argues that investment in cycling infrastructure will lead more people to cycle. Do you think the government has a responsibility to change how citizens travel, or should individuals be free to choose their own transport? Where should the line be drawn between public health policy and personal freedom?
